title: Pressure-Temperature Phase Diagram and $λ$-Transition in Liquid Sulfur
authors: Sonia Salomoni, Frédéric Datchi, A. Marco Saitta, Arthur France-Lanord
abstract: Using molecular dynamics simulations driven by a machine-learned interatomic potential, we investigate at low to intermediate pressures the $λ$-transition of sulfur, a temperature-induced polymerization. At ambient pressure, we capture the melting of crystalline cyclo-octasulfur into a liquid of molecular rings. Within this liquid, the concentration of non-S$_8$ rings increases with temperature; we show that these molecules act as reactive centers, which eventually trigger polymerization. We reproduce key experimental signatures of the $λ$-transition, including the sharp increase in heat capacity and the pronounced dependence of the transition temperature on the heating rate. Building on this, we reconstruct a phase diagram of polymerization up to intermediate pressures. Our results reveal a moderate decrease of the polymerization temperature with pressure, culminating with its merging with the melting line at a critical point. Beyond this point, we provide direct evidence of polymerization emerging from the crystalline phase. By analyzing temperature-ramp trajectories, we observe the formation of non-S$_8$ rings, open chains, and extended polymeric structures which retain features of the crystalline arrangement; further heating the system leads to disorder taking over through melting. Polymerization is therefore initiated slightly before melting. Altogether, our findings provide a microscopic picture of the $λ$-transition throughout the sulfur phase diagram.
summary: This paper uses machine-learned molecular dynamics to provide a microscopic view of the polymerization transition in sulfur. It reveals that non-S8 rings act as the primary triggers for the formation of long polymer chains. Crucially, the study shows that at high pressures, the polymerization process begins within the solid crystal before melting occurs. The work successfully reconstructs the sulfur phase diagram, including the merging of the melting and polymerization lines at a critical point.
main_problem: Understanding the atomistic mechanisms of the lambda-transition (temperature-induced polymerization) in liquid sulfur and reconstructing its pressure-temperature phase diagram.
main_result: The study identifies non-S8 rings as the reactive seeds for polymerization and demonstrates that at high pressures, polymerization and melting lines merge, with polymerization initiating within the crystal lattice.
method: Large-scale molecular dynamics simulations driven by a local equivariant machine-learned interatomic potential (Allegro model) trained via active learning from DFT data.
model_or_system: Elemental sulfur, specifically focusing on the transitions between crystalline alpha-S8, molecular liquid (S8 rings), and polymeric liquid (chains/rings) under varying temperature and pressure.
key_observables: Atomic coordination, ring size distribution (S8, S-pi, S-infinity), potential energy, heat capacity, volume, density, diffusivity, and viscosity.
important_parameters: Pressure up to 1.0 GPa, temperature range 300 K to 900 K, and various heating rates for temperature ramps.
main_assumptions: The simulation assumes a fixed singlet multiplicity, which prevents the accurate description of isolated sulfur atoms; a coordination constraint was used to avoid non-physical single-atom fragments.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the visual evolution of S8 crystal heating and atom lineage; Figure 2 displays evolution of potential energy, volume, and ring content during temperature ramps; Figure 3 analyzes the 1.0 GPa trajectory showing lattice-conforming polymers.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the lambda-transition problem, describes the development and validation of the machine-learned potential, presents the reconstruction of the phase diagram, details the microscopic polymerization mechanism, and discusses high-pressure coupling and limitations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 3. Analysis of the trajectory at 1.0 GPa with 50 K/ns heat- ing rate. (a) Visual representation of small rings, small polymers and long polymers conforming to the lattice in the pre-melting regime. The non-S8 fragments are highlighted with pink and purple colors. (b) SOAP vector similarity for S8, small and long polymers in a se- lected temperature range across melting. The vertical line marks the sharp increase in polymeric content and the concurrent broadening of the SOAP similarity distribution.

FIG. 4. Experimental and computational phase diagrams. Ex- perimental data points are taken from references: and [58] (see [20]), [19], [21], [24], [1]. The black solid lines are interpolations of the experimental data, and the dotted line is the the- oretical prediction from [20]. The symbol reports the extrapolated computational polymerization temperature. The other symbols mark the onset and endset of polymerization and melting obtained during T-ramp simulations at 50 K/ns. Dashed lines are obtained from the linear fit or spline interpolation of these points.
